questions.question: Explain the five stages of the classic RISC instruction cycle. For each stage, briefly describe its primary function in executing a typical arithmetic or logical instruction.
keyPoints: IF (Instruction Fetch): Fetches instruction from memory using PC
ID (Instruction Decode): Decodes instruction and reads registers
EX (Execute): Performs arithmetic/logical operation or address calculation
MEM (Memory Access): Accesses data memory for load/store instructions
WB (Write Back): Writes result back to destination register

questions.question: A 5-stage RISC pipeline processes 1000 instructions. The pipeline has a fill latency of 4 cycles and a drain latency of 4 cycles. Each pipeline stage takes 1 clock cycle. Calculate: (a) Total clock cycles required, (b) Effective CPI, and (c) Speedup over a non-pipelined processor (assuming non-pipelined CPI = 5).

(a) With the help of a diagram, illustrate how a RAW (Read After Write) data hazard occurs in a 5-stage pipeline when executing the instructions: I1: ADD R1, R2, R3 and I2: SUB R4, R1, R5. (b) Explain how hardware forwarding (also called bypass) resolves this hazard. (c) What is the difference between stall-based and delay slot-based approaches for handling control hazards in pipeline?
keyPoints: RAW hazard: I2 needs R1 value in EX stage but I1 writes it in WB stage (without forwarding, 2-cycle stall needed)
Forwarding: Route result directly from EX/MEM and MEM/WB pipeline registers to EX stage of dependent instruction
Stall-based: Insert bubble/NOPs until hazard resolves (wastes cycles)
Delay slot: Fill with independent instructions (compiler's responsibility, no wasted cycles)

questions.question: Consider a 5-stage pipeline processor with branch prediction. Assume: (a) 20% of instructions are branches, (b) Branch misprediction penalty is 3 cycles, (c) Static branch prediction (always not taken) accuracy is 60%. Calculate: (i) Overall CPI with no branch prediction, (ii) CPI with static prediction assuming 60% accuracy, (iii) CPI assuming a dynamic 2-bit saturating counter predictor achieves 85% accuracy. Also explain how 2-bit branch prediction works and discuss one advantage and one limitation of dynamic branch predictors.
keyPoints: 2-bit predictor: Uses saturating counter (00=strongly not taken to 11=strongly taken), updates based on branch outcome; Advantage: adapts to program behavior; Limitation: requires additional hardware and training time
